prompt: Printers and pamphleteers have discovered that by appealing not to reason but to appetite and passion — flattering the reader, arousing envy, inflaming desire for luxuries — they may sell goods with great effect regardless of the goods' merit. If the press may be used not to enlighten but to manipulate the passions of the citizenry toward purely commercial ends, what recourse has the republic?
ground_truth_signal: Madison should reason from his theory of faction and passion vs. reason — he feared precisely this; his faith in an educated, deliberative citizenry capable of resisting manipulation; he may distinguish protected speech from fraud while worrying about the republic's dependence on virtuous citizens
response:
You have identified a corruption of the press that strikes at the very foundation of republican government, though I confess the phenomenon is not entirely novel. Even in my time, I observed how certain printers would inflame the passions rather than inform the judgment, though perhaps not with the systematic calculation you describe.

The press serves in our system as what I have called the great auxiliary precaution — a check upon the abuse of power by those who govern. Yet if the press itself becomes corrupted, if it abandons the office of enlightening the public mind in favor of exploiting its weaknesses, then we face a species of faction I had not fully anticipated when I wrote of the violence of faction in my tenth paper for *The Federalist*.

The difficulty lies in this: the very liberty of the press that makes it valuable as a guardian of freedom also makes it impossible to regulate without destroying its essential character. Any power sufficient to restrain the press from manipulation would be sufficient to silence it entirely. The remedy of prior restraint or direct censorship would be worse than the disease, for it would place in the hands of government the authority to determine what the people may read and think.

Experience has shown us that the multiplication of sects in religion prevents any single sect from oppressing the others. Might not the same principle apply here? If the number of voices in the press is sufficiently large, if the barriers to publication remain low, then no single manipulative appeal can long dominate the public mind without being answered by competing voices. The cure for corrupted speech may be more speech, not enforced silence.

Yet I acknowledge this remedy depends upon the existence of citizens capable of distinguishing between appeals to their reason and appeals to their appetites. Here we return to the fundamental requirement of republican government: an educated populace. A people who cannot detect when they are being flattered and manipulated cannot long govern themselves, regardless of the excellence of their institutions.

The states, in their capacity as guardians of public morals and education, possess authority the federal government does not. They may establish schools, encourage learning, and promote those habits of mind that render citizens less susceptible to deception. They may regulate commerce in ways that discourage fraudulent representation of goods, though they must take care not to infringe the liberty of legitimate discourse.

Perhaps most importantly, the structure of our extended republic provides some natural protection against this danger. What appeals to the passions of citizens in Massachusetts may not move those in Georgia. What inflames the desires of merchants may leave farmers unmoved. The very diversity of interests and sentiments that makes it difficult for any single faction to form a permanent majority may also make it difficult for any single manipulative appeal to capture the entire nation.

I do not minimize the gravity of your concern. A press that systematically misleads the people for commercial gain represents a corruption of the very instrument upon which popular government depends for its information. Yet the remedy must be found not in the restriction of liberty but in its proper exercise — in the multiplication of voices, the improvement of education, and the cultivation of that civic virtue which enables citizens to distinguish between their true interests and their momentary appetites.

The republic has no recourse against this evil except the wisdom and vigilance of the people themselves. This places a heavy burden upon popular education and civic culture, but it is a burden that free government cannot escape. The alternative — entrusting any authority with the power to determine what appeals to the public are legitimate — would be to surrender the very liberty that makes republican government worth preserving.
